museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: December brings Zelda to PAL players and PC RPG depth.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for December 1998. Dates are deliberately conservative where regional timing is uncertain.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched month markers or context notes.

items.title: Ocarina of Time reaches PAL regions in December.
items.body: A month marker with regional timing kept visible rather than smoothed into a single global date.

items.title: UK Christmas lists now include N64 epics, PlayStation staples and PC boxes.
items.body: From a UK-aware angle, this mattered through delayed releases, import pages, demo discs and high-street availability.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: 1998 ends feeling like too much history for one shelf.
items.body: A researched period marker, written cautiously where sources disagree or the month is more atmosphere than hard date.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Baldur's Gate
items.body: BioWare restores party-based D&D to the late-90s PC shelf.
items.sourceNote: December 1998 release is widely cited.

items.title: Resident Evil 2
items.body: Capcom moves survival horror from mansion to city-scale panic.
items.sourceNote: January 1998 date is widely cited.

items.title: StarCraft
items.body: Blizzard turns asymmetric RTS design into a multiplayer language.
items.sourceNote: StarCraft sources cite March 31, 1998.

items.title: Unreal
items.body: Epic's shooter makes lighting, scale and engine technology part of the PC boast.
items.sourceNote: May 1998 release is commonly cited.

items.title: Banjo-Kazooie
items.body: Rare gives the N64 a warm, dense collectathon with British studio fingerprints.
items.sourceNote: June 1998 North American date is widely cited.

items.title: Metal Gear Solid
items.body: Kojima turns stealth, cinema and controller tricks into a PlayStation event.
items.sourceNote: Japanese date is source-cited; Western dates follow.

items.title: Pokemon Red and Blue
items.body: Pokemon reaches North America and starts building the Western wave that hits Europe in 1999.
items.sourceNote: Bulbapedia cites North America September 28, 1998.

items.title: Half-Life
items.body: Valve stitches FPS action into a continuous authored disaster.
items.sourceNote: Steam lists November 19, 1998.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, media and platform context around the month.

items.title: Game Boy Color launches in Japan
items.body: Nintendo refreshes handheld play just as Pokemon is beginning its Western climb.
items.sourceNote: October 21, 1998 Japanese context; Western launches follow.

items.title: Dreamcast launches in Japan
items.body: Sega opens the sixth generation with a built-in modem and arcade-derived confidence.
items.sourceNote: November 27, 1998 Japanese launch is source-cited.

items.title: N64 peaks with event software
items.body: Ocarina of Time makes the cartridge machine feel essential again, even beside PlayStation volume.
items.sourceNote: Ocarina 1998 context.

items.title: PC gaming splits between strategy, shooter and RPG
items.body: StarCraft, Half-Life, Unreal, Thief and Baldur's Gate show several PC futures at once.
items.sourceNote: 1998 PC release context.

items.title: Edge
items.headline: Edge on the 1998 shelf
items.curatorNote: A year where serious criticism had to make room for several all-time landmarks at once.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How network play, downloads, fan pages and demo culture felt at the time.

items.title: Battle.net becomes routine for strategy players
items.body: StarCraft turns online matchmaking and ladder identity into part of the game.

items.title: Half-Life mod culture is waiting in the walls
items.body: Valve's shooter arrives as a platform for communities, tools and eventually Counter-Strike.

items.title: Pokemon trading remains mostly physical
items.body: The Western wave begins, but playground cables and local friends still matter more than websites.

items.title: Dreamcast makes online console play imaginable
items.body: The built-in modem is not yet a UK reality, but the idea changes how Sega talks about the future.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of history.

items.title: Every platform had a masterpiece argument
items.body: N64, PlayStation and PC could each point to a different future and sound convincing.

items.title: The year felt overcrowded in the best way
items.body: One magazine issue could hold Zelda, Metal Gear, Half-Life and StarCraft without blinking.

items.title: Pokemon was approaching Britain from overseas
items.body: UK players could feel the wave coming through American coverage and import chatter.

items.title: Sega seemed to be preparing one last clean slate
items.body: Dreamcast made Saturn's troubles look like prelude rather than ending.
